title: I wanted to build vertical SaaS for pest control, so I took a technician job
summary: A developer quit his tech job and spent 3 months working as a pest control technician to deeply understand the industry before building software for it. Now his SaaS is growing fast because he truly understands his customers' pain points.
comment: This is the most underrated indie dev strategy. Most failed SaaS products die because the founder builds what they THINK customers want. Taking a job in your target industry costs you 3 months but saves you years of building the wrong thing. The best vertical SaaS founders are industry insiders first, developers second.

title: I replaced a $120/year micro-SaaS in 20 minutes with LLM-generated code
summary: Pragmatic Engineer's Gergely Orosz used an LLM to rewrite a SaaS tool he'd been paying $120/year for. The SaaS hadn't shipped new features in 4 years and had a broken billing system for 3 years.
comment: Wake-up call for every indie SaaS founder: your moat is NOT your code anymore. If your product is a thin wrapper around basic logic with no unique data or network effects, AI will eat you alive. The new survival playbook: either go deep into a niche with proprietary workflows, or build community/data moats that can't be replicated in 20 minutes.

title: Cloudflare rewrites Next.js — AI is disrupting open source business models
summary: A single Cloudflare engineer rewrote most of Vercel's Next.js in one week using AI agents. This signals a massive shift: AI is eroding the moats of commercial open source companies.
comment: This changes the calculus for indie devs choosing a tech stack. If a giant like Cloudflare can rewrite your framework dependency in a week, what happens when they decide to compete with YOUR product? Takeaway: don't build on top of frameworks where the hosting company IS the business model. Own your deployment, own your data, own your distribution.

title: StageTimer.io — Making $8k/mo from a niche countdown timer
summary: Lukas and Liz Hermann built StageTimer.io, a simple countdown timer for live events, churches, and conferences. $8k/month revenue, two-person team, targeting a $100M/year industry nobody thinks about.
comment: The unsexy SaaS playbook in action. A countdown timer sounds trivial until you realize: event organizers NEED reliability, they'll pay monthly without blinking, and they tell other event organizers about it. $8k/mo from a 'boring' tool beats $0/mo from your AI startup. When picking a niche, ask: is this boring enough that no VC-backed company will bother competing?

title: Bolt.new — Build and deploy full-stack apps from a prompt
summary: StackBlitz's AI dev tool lets you describe what you want in plain English and get a running full-stack web app with Node.js backend, database, and one-click deployment. No local setup needed.
comment: This is the tool that makes 'build in a weekend, launch on Monday' actually real. For indie devs, the strategic use isn't building your main product — it's rapid prototyping. Spin up 5 different MVPs in a day, throw them on Product Hunt, see which one gets traction, then rebuild the winner properly. The cost of testing ideas just dropped to near zero.

title: Polar.sh — Finally, a real way to monetize open source
summary: Polar provides subscriptions, paid downloads, and license management for open source developers. Hundreds of projects already use it to turn GitHub stars into actual revenue.
comment: If you have an open source project with 500+ stars, you're leaving money on the table by not trying Polar. The traditional 'open core' model requires building enterprise features. Polar's approach is simpler: let your existing fans pay for what they already use. Even 1% of your star-gazers paying $10/mo adds up fast. Stars are vanity, revenue is sanity.

title: Pieter Levels: $200k+/month across 7 products, zero employees
summary: The legendary indie maker behind NomadList, RemoteOK, and PhotoAI shares his methodology: vanilla tech stack (jQuery + PHP), ship fast, let users guide the roadmap, automate everything.
comment: Levels is proof that you don't need React, microservices, or a team. His secret isn't technical — it's operational: he treats every product as a 'bet', kills losers fast, and doubles down on winners. The real lesson most people miss: he doesn't build what's cool, he builds what people already pay for elsewhere but worse. Find existing spend, make it better.

title: Coolify — Self-hosted Vercel, fully open source
summary: Deploy any app to your own server with one click. Docker, databases, auto SSL, CI/CD. 35k+ GitHub stars. Perfect for indie devs who want to escape $20/mo Vercel bills that scale to $200/mo overnight.
comment: Hosting costs are the silent killer of indie projects. Vercel/Netlify are great until you get traffic and the bill explodes. A $5/mo VPS + Coolify gives you more power and predictable costs. The 30 minutes you spend setting it up saves you from platform lock-in and surprise invoices. Every serious indie dev should know how to self-host.

title: Tauri 2.0 — Cross-platform desktop + mobile, 1/10 of Electron's size
summary: Tauri 2.0 adds iOS and Android support. Build native apps using web tech with a Rust backend. Package sizes under 5MB vs Electron's 100MB+. Memory usage is dramatically lower.
comment: Desktop apps are an underrated indie opportunity. The market is less crowded than web SaaS, users expect to pay more (one-time $29-99 vs $5/mo subscription), and distribution through app stores provides organic discovery. Tauri 2.0 with mobile support makes this even more compelling. Consider: your next side project could be a desktop app.

title: Chrome extension to $5k MRR in 6 months — the playbook
summary: An indie dev shares the full playbook: find a pain point on Twitter, build a Chrome extension MVP in a weekend, launch on Product Hunt, iterate on feedback. 15k free users, 3% paid conversion at $5/mo.
comment: Chrome extensions are the best-kept secret in indie dev. They're cheap to build, the Chrome Web Store gives you free distribution, and 3% conversion is achievable. The math: 15,000 free users x 3% x $5/mo = $2,250/mo. Get to 30k users and you're at $5k MRR. The key insight: solve ONE specific pain point perfectly. Don't build a Swiss army knife.

title: Hono — The 14KB framework conquering edge computing
summary: Ultra-lightweight web framework that runs everywhere: Cloudflare Workers, Deno, Bun, Node.js. Built-in JSX, middleware, and type safety. Growing fast as the default for edge-first projects.
comment: If you're starting a new API or lightweight web project in 2026, Hono deserves serious consideration over Express. It's faster, smaller, and runs on any runtime. For indie devs, the killer feature is Cloudflare Workers deployment: your API runs globally on 300+ locations with a generous free tier. Zero cold starts, zero DevOps.

title: Email.md — Markdown to email-safe HTML
summary: Write emails in Markdown, get responsive HTML that works in every email client. 246 points on Hacker News. A Show HN project that solves a real, everyday pain point.
comment: This is a textbook example of a great side project: it solves a specific annoying problem, it's simple enough to build in a weekend, and the target audience (developers, marketers) is huge. Notice it doesn't try to be an email platform — it does ONE thing. The best side projects are painkillers, not vitamins. What's YOUR 'Markdown to X' idea?

title: The grief when AI writes most of the code
summary: A thoughtful piece on what happens to software engineers when AI writes 80%+ of the code. Where does the value shift? What skills matter now?
comment: The developers who will thrive aren't the fastest coders — they're the ones who understand WHAT to build and WHY. AI commoditizes implementation but amplifies judgment. For indie devs, this is actually great news: the gap between 'idea' and 'working product' is shrinking to hours. Your competitive advantage is now taste, domain knowledge, and distribution — not technical skill.

title: Seth Godin on indie hacking and finding significance
summary: Marketing legend Seth Godin discusses finding business ideas by being useful, the difference between freelancing and bootstrapping, and how to build something meaningful in the age of AI.
comment: Godin's core message for indie devs: stop asking 'what can I build?' and start asking 'who can I serve?' The most successful indie products aren't technically impressive — they're deeply empathetic to a specific group of people. Build for a tribe you understand, charge them fairly, and treat every customer like they matter. Marketing is just the act of being genuinely useful.

title: Supabase Edge Functions now support Python
summary: Write serverless backend logic in Python on Supabase. Combined with their free PostgreSQL database, auth, and storage, it's arguably the best free backend-as-a-service for indie developers.
comment: The free tier stack for 2026: Supabase (database + auth + edge functions) + Vercel/Cloudflare (frontend hosting) + Resend (email). Total cost: $0 until you have real traction. There's no longer any excuse for 'I need funding to build an MVP.' Ship first, pay later. If you can't get users on a free stack, paying for infrastructure won't help.
